refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and real-time feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit connection to the mechanisms (monitoring, control, feedback) that enable efficiency achievement. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is not a passive property but an active capability requiring measurement, optimization logic, and operator awareness. This supports both direct operation and teleoperation modes.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = total_energy_used / total_ton_moved over the evaluation period. Verify that measured value ≤ E_target. Conduct trials across representative worksite conditions, load profiles, and operator skill levels. Establish baseline and delta analysis.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) and work output (tons moved) with sufficient granularity to compute energy_per_ton.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time feedback to operators (direct and tele-operators) on current energy efficiency status and deviations from optimal efficiency targets.

CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall implement control strategies (e.g., load optimization, route planning, idle reduction, transmission efficiency) that minimize energy consumption while maintaining safety and productivity.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall support post-evaluation analysis and reporting of energy_per_ton KPI against E_target for acceptance verification.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Passive energy monitoring only (measure and log, no active optimization or feedback)
DP-B: Active energy management system with real-time feedback, predictive control, and operator guidance integrated across vehicle and teleoperation subsystems
DP-C: Centralized energy optimization engine with remote advisory (tele-operator provides efficiency guidance post-hoc)
DP-D: Distributed energy optimization with local vehicle autonomy and remote telemetry aggregation
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Active energy management system with real-time feedback, predictive control, and operator guidance integrated across vehicle and teleoperation subsystems

potential_sources: Integration between Vehicle control logic and Teleoperation feedback channels (Telemetry interface carries energy state; Tasking interface carries operator commands that influence efficiency)
Safety criticality (high) couples with energy optimization: aggressive efficiency strategies must not compromise safety margins; SafetyAlert interface must gate efficiency recommendations
Network latency in teleoperation may degrade real-time feedback effectiveness; mitigation via local vehicle autonomy
coupling_check.notes: DP-B is selected because it directly addresses the refined FR's requirement for active optimization and feedback. Passive monitoring (DP-A) cannot achieve efficiency targets without operator guidance. DP-C introduces latency risk. DP-D is viable but DP-B's integrated approach is more aligned with the high safety criticality constraint.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle control systems can be augmented with energy optimization logic without compromising existing safety interlocks.
Assumption: Teleoperation network bandwidth and latency support real-time energy feedback (Telemetry interface).
Risk: If energy optimization logic conflicts with safety constraints, efficiency gains may be negated; mitigation requires explicit safety-efficiency trade-off analysis.
Risk: Operator behavior (skill, attention) significantly influences energy efficiency; system design must account for variability in operator adoption and compliance.
Risk: Energy target E_target may be unrealistic for certain worksite conditions; acceptance criteria must include conditional clauses or sensitivity analysis.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the priority hierarchy: safety > efficiency. Specify that energy optimization strategies must be validated against safety acceptance criteria before deployment. Define fallback modes if real-time feedback is unavailable (e.g., local vehicle autonomy defaults to conservative efficiency mode).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary encompassing all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and their integration. At this decomposition level, the IM assignment reflects that energy efficiency is a system-level property requiring coordination across all subsystems. The NGHE Solution IM owns the energy management system architecture, including measurement, feedback, and control integration.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution transmits real-time energy consumption data (kWh, power draw, efficiency metrics) and work output (tons moved, cycle time) to external monitoring systems and tele-operators. Supports energy_per_ton computation and feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives operator commands (direct and tele-operator) that influence energy consumption (e.g., load size, route, speed profile). Tasking interface carries efficiency-relevant parameters.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives safety constraints and alerts that gate energy optimization strategies. Transmits alerts if energy optimization strategies approach safety limits. Ensures safety-efficiency trade-offs are managed.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link for teleoperation connectivity, enabling real-time feedback and remote operator guidance on energy efficiency. Latency and bandwidth constraints affect feedback effectiveness.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level boundary. Energy efficiency is a system-level property requiring integration across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Containment is high because the energy management system architecture, measurement framework, and feedback integration are owned at this level. Minimal diffusion: specific implementations (e.g., vehicle control algorithms) are delegated to subsystems but coordinated here.

Vehicle subsystem contains the primary energy consumption mechanisms (engine, transmission, hydraulics) and local control logic. Energy optimization strategies (load balancing, idle reduction, transmission efficiency) are implemented in Vehicle. However, Vehicle alone cannot achieve system-level energy efficiency without feedback from Teleoperation and Safety Services; diffusion risk is moderate.

Teleoperation subsystem provides real-time feedback to tele-operators and can influence operator behavior (e.g., efficiency guidance, load recommendations). However, Teleoperation is not the primary locus of energy optimization logic; it is a feedback and guidance channel. Diffusion risk is higher because energy efficiency depends on operator compliance and behavior change.

Safety Services constrains energy optimization strategies (e.g., prevents aggressive efficiency modes that reduce safety margins). Safety Services does not directly implement energy optimization but acts as a gating mechanism. Containment is moderate; diffusion risk is high because safety-efficiency trade-offs are distributed across multiple subsystems.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: NGHE Solution (system-level integration owner) with primary implementation responsibility delegated to Vehicle (energy optimization logic) and Teleoperation (feedback and operator guidance). Safety Services provides constraint validation.
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Energy efficiency is a system-level emergent property that cannot be fully contained within a single subsystem. The NGHE Solution IM owns the architecture and integration framework; Vehicle owns the primary optimization mechanisms; Teleoperation owns the feedback and guidance mechanisms; Safety Services owns the constraint validation. This distributed ownership is acceptable if interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clearly defined and integration points are explicit.
containment_risks: Risk: Energy optimization logic may be duplicated across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, leading to inconsistency and maintenance burden. Mitigation: Establish a single source of truth for energy optimization algorithms, owned by Vehicle, with Teleoperation consuming results via Telemetry interface.

Risk: Real-time feedback loop (Telemetry -> Teleoperation -> Tasking -> Vehicle) may introduce latency that degrades efficiency. Mitigation: Implement local vehicle autonomy (fallback mode) that operates independently of teleoperation feedback.

Risk: Safety-efficiency trade-offs may not be resolved consistently across subsystems. Mitigation: Establish explicit safety-efficiency priority rules and validate all optimization strategies against SafetyAlert constraints before deployment.

Risk: Operator behavior variability may dominate energy efficiency outcomes, making system-level optimization ineffective. Mitigation: Design feedback mechanisms (Telemetry interface) to influence operator behavior; measure and report operator compliance.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the IM assignment by explicitly defining the energy management system architecture at the NGHE Solution level, including: (1) measurement framework (what is measured, where, how often), (2) optimization logic ownership (Vehicle subsystem), (3) feedback mechanism (Telemetry interface), (4) operator guidance (Teleoperation subsystem), (5) safety constraint validation (Safety Services). Create a detailed interface specification for Telemetry (energy metrics), Tasking (efficiency-relevant parameters), and SafetyAlert (efficiency-safety trade-offs).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (Active energy management system with real-time feedback and predictive control) directly addresses the refined FR's requirements for continuous measurement, real-time feedback, control optimization, and post-evaluation analysis. The DP is neither over-specified (avoids prescribing implementation details) nor under-specified (provides sufficient guidance for subsystem decomposition). Coupling between FR and DP is acceptable given the high safety criticality constraint and the explicit safety-efficiency trade-off analysis in the DP assumptions.

diffusion_risks: Energy optimization logic is distributed across Vehicle (primary implementation) and Teleoperation (feedback and guidance). Mitigation: Establish clear ownership and interface contracts.
Real-time feedback loop introduces latency risk in teleoperation mode. Mitigation: Implement local vehicle autonomy fallback.
Safety-efficiency trade-offs are managed across multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Safety Services). Mitigation: Establish explicit priority rules and validation procedures.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution IM is the appropriate system-level boundary for energy efficiency. The distributed ownership across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services is acceptable if interfaces are well-defined. Containment score for NGHE Solution is 95, indicating strong alignment. Diffusion risks are moderate and manageable through explicit interface specifications and integration testing.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-003 is feasible through field trials measuring energy_per_ton over the evaluation period. Sub-FRs (measurement, feedback, control, reporting) are independently verifiable. Acceptance criteria (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) are measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of energy efficiency responsibility is clear: NGHE Solution owns the system-level architecture and integration; Vehicle owns optimization logic; Teleoperation owns feedback and guidance; Safety Services owns constraint validation. Each subsystem has a defined role in achieving the energy efficiency target.

recommended_revisions: Refine the energy target E_target definition: specify conditions (worksite type, load profile, operator skill level) under which the target applies. Include sensitivity analysis or conditional acceptance criteria.
Establish explicit safety-efficiency priority rules: define which safety constraints are non-negotiable and which can be relaxed for efficiency gains (if any).

Define the measurement framework: specify what is measured (energy consumption, work output), where (vehicle sensors, teleoperation system), how often (sampling rate), and how it is aggregated (energy_per_ton computation).
Specify the real-time feedback mechanism: define latency requirements, data format, and operator interface for energy efficiency feedback in both direct and teleoperation modes.
Plan for operator behavior analysis: design mechanisms to measure and report operator compliance with efficiency guidance; include training and incentive strategies.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR has four sub-FRs (measurement, feedback, control, reporting) that must be individually designed and allocated to subsystems. The selected DP (active energy management system) is sound but requires detailed specification of measurement framework, feedback mechanisms, control strategies, and safety-efficiency trade-off rules. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution with distributed ownership) is appropriate at the system level but requires explicit interface specifications and integration architecture. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify subsystem responsibilities and enable detailed design of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services contributions to energy efficiency.
decision.justification_detail: The gate review identified no blocking issues (all gates pass), but recommended revisions indicate that the current level of specification is insufficient for detailed design. The sub-FRs are well-defined and ready for individual decomposition. The DP is sound but requires refinement of safety-efficiency trade-off rules. The IM assignment is appropriate but requires explicit interface specifications. Proceeding to ZIGZAG_DOWN will address these refinements at the subsystem level.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) and work output (tons moved) with sufficient granularity to compute energy_per_ton.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time feedback to operators (direct and tele-operators) on current energy efficiency status and deviations from optimal efficiency targets.

CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall implement control strategies (e.g., load optimization, route planning, idle reduction, transmission efficiency) that minimize energy consumption while maintaining safety and productivity.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall support post-evaluation analysis and reporting of energy_per_ton KPI against E_target for acceptance verification.

assumptions: Vehicle control systems can be augmented with energy optimization logic without compromising existing safety interlocks.
Teleoperation network bandwidth and latency support real-time energy feedback (Telemetry interface).
Energy target E_target is achievable under representative worksite conditions with the selected DP.
Operator behavior (skill, attention) can be influenced through real-time feedback and guidance mechanisms.
Safety-efficiency trade-offs can be resolved through explicit priority rules and constraint validation.
Measurement framework (sensors, data aggregation) is available or can be implemented in Vehicle subsystem.
open_risks: Energy optimization logic may conflict with safety constraints, negating efficiency gains. Mitigation: Establish explicit safety-efficiency priority rules and validate all optimization strategies against SafetyAlert constraints.
Real-time feedback loop may introduce latency that degrades efficiency in teleoperation mode. Mitigation: Implement local vehicle autonomy fallback mode.
Operator behavior variability may dominate energy efficiency outcomes. Mitigation: Design feedback mechanisms to influence operator behavior; measure and report compliance.
Energy target E_target may be unrealistic for certain worksite conditions. Mitigation: Include conditional acceptance criteria or sensitivity analysis.
Distributed ownership of energy efficiency across multiple subsystems may lead to inconsistency or gaps. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contracts and integration testing procedures.
evidence_needed: Field trial data: energy_per_ton measurements over the evaluation period under representative worksite conditions.
Safety analysis: validation that energy optimization strategies do not compromise safety margins.
Operator feedback: qualitative and quantitative data on operator perception of real-time feedback and guidance mechanisms.
Network performance data: latency and bandwidth measurements for teleoperation feedback loop.
Baseline and delta analysis: comparison of energy efficiency with and without the active energy management system.
Detailed interface specifications: Telemetry (energy metrics), Tasking (efficiency-relevant parameters), SafetyAlert (efficiency-safety trade-offs), Network Link (latency, bandwidth).
